meta.title: Cabinet Refinishing Planning Guide — Jerry & Co.
meta.description: Everything to check, decide, and expect before refinishing your condo cabinets. Self-assessment, decision worksheet, day-by-day workbook, and warranty terms.

hero.eyebrow: Your condo kitchen — before you call anyone.
hero.headline: Everything you need to know before refinishing your condo cabinets
hero.subhead: This guide walks you through what to check, what to decide, and what to expect — so your estimate call is fast, your project runs smoothly, and the finish lasts.

selfAssessment.heading: 5 questions before you reach out
selfAssessment.intro: Answer these honestly. They'll tell you whether refinishing is right for your cabinets — and flag anything to discuss on the estimate call.

questions.q: Are your cabinet boxes structurally sound — no soft wood, delamination, or water damage at the base?
questions.label: Good to go
questions.hint: Box integrity is the foundation. If yes, refinishing makes sense.

questions.q: Are your doors a flat or simple routed profile — not heavily carved, glass-front, or thermofoil wrap?
questions.label: Discuss on call
questions.hint: Most profiles work. Thermofoil and intricate carved doors need a closer look — we'll assess on the video walkthrough.

questions.q: Does your building allow contractor access during standard weekday hours (8am–5pm)?
questions.label: Confirm first
questions.hint: Access hours and elevator reservation windows affect scheduling. Get this from your condo docs before booking.

questions.q: Are you comfortable being without your kitchen for 2–3 consecutive days during the project?
questions.label: Good to go
questions.hint: That's the typical production window. Planning around it makes everything easier.

questions.q: Do you want a lasting result built for daily use — not just a cosmetic fix that needs touching up in a year?
questions.label: Right service
questions.hint: 2K polyurethane is harder and more durable than most brush-on paints. If durability matters, this is the right call.

selfAssessment.outcomeNote: Mostly green → strong candidate. Yellow items → bring them to the video walkthrough, no commitment needed. Red on box condition → let's talk honestly about whether repair or replacement makes more sense first.

preplanning.heading: What to document before the walkthrough
preplanning.intro: You don't need all of this locked down — but clients who come prepared get a written quote faster and avoid scope surprises.

categories.title: Your cabinets
items: Count total door and drawer front count
Photograph each run front-on in good light
Close-up photos of any chipped edges, worn corners, or staining
Note whether boxes have existing paint or stain (affects prep)

categories.title: Your building
items: Check condo bylaws for contractor access hours and elevator reservation process
Confirm whether your building requires a certificate of insurance from contractors
Note any shared ventilation restrictions — relevant for low-VOC finish selection
Find out if neighboring units will need notice for any reason

categories.title: Your timeline
items: Identify any hard date constraints — guests arriving, listing date, lease renewal
Confirm 2–5 consecutive weekdays are available for the project
Decide if you'll be present during work or provide key access
Note any travel or building blackout periods coming up

preplanning.callout: Clients who send 4–6 photos before the walkthrough call typically receive their written quote the same day.

decisions.heading: Decisions you'll make before work begins
decisions.intro: None of these need to be answered before the estimate call — but knowing what's coming helps you think them through.

items.label: Color
items.question: What color do you want the finished cabinets?
options: Bone white (most popular in Boston condos)
Deep forest green
Matte black
Custom color match to an existing element
items.jerrySays: Start with the countertop and hardware finish, then build the cabinet color around those. Bone white reads lighter in person than on a screen — always request a sample before committing.

items.label: Sheen level
items.question: How much sheen do you want on the finished surface?
options: Matte — lowest sheen, hides surface texture
Satin — most popular, easy to wipe clean
Semi-gloss — highest durability, shows imperfections more
items.jerrySays: Satin is the right call for 80% of kitchens. Matte looks elegant but shows fingerprints and is harder to wipe. Semi-gloss is worth it for high-traffic households.

items.label: Hardware
items.question: Are you keeping your existing hardware, upgrading, or replacing it?
options: Keep existing hardware (reinstalled after finishing)
Upgrade to a new finish (e.g. brass to matte black)
Full replacement — new pulls and knobs
items.jerrySays: If replacing, order hardware before the project starts — lead times catch people off guard. Measure hole-to-hole spacing on your current pulls before ordering anything new.

items.label: Scope
items.question: Which surfaces are included in this project?
options: Kitchen doors and drawer fronts only
Doors, drawer fronts, and visible cabinet boxes
Kitchen plus bathroom vanity or laundry cabinet in same session
items.jerrySays: Bundling the bathroom vanity in the same project visit is the most efficient way to get consistent color across your home. It adds minimal time and typically less than $600 to the total.

phases.label: Before we arrive
items: Written quote reviewed and signed
Color and sheen level locked — no changes after this point
Hardware ordered if replacing (allow 5–10 business days)
Building elevator and access window reserved
Pre-arrival checklist sent — you'll clear cabinet contents and leave key access

phases.label: Days 1–5 in your unit
items: Day 1: Hardware removed, doors labeled, doors transported off-site for prep
Day 1–2: Hand-sanding with cork blocks — no power tools, no noise through walls
Day 2: Chips, dents, and edge damage filled and leveled
Day 3–4: 2K polyurethane applied in two controlled coats, off-site spray environment
Day 5: Doors returned, hardware reinstalled, alignment checked

phases.label: After sign-off
items: 48-hour cure window — avoid wiping or placing items against doors during this period
Final walkthrough together — you inspect every door, edge, and hinge point
Touch-up items addressed before you sign off, not after
Building hallway and elevator inspected and cleared before departure
Written warranty issued with your sign-off confirmation

outcomes.heading: What 'done' actually looks like
gets: Factory-smooth, consistent finish across every door and drawer front
2K polyurethane surface harder and more durable than most brush-on paints
Hardware aligned and functioning the same as or better than before
Zero hallway damage, zero dust migration to other rooms
A written sign-off with a 2-year workmanship warranty
know: Open-grain wood (oak, ash) will show the grain through the finish — that's normal and expected, not a defect
Deep structural damage to boxes or doors must be repaired before finishing — we assess and flag this in the walkthrough
Color will appear slightly different under warm incandescent vs. cool natural light — always sample in your actual space
The first 48 hours after application are the critical cure window — the finish is touch-dry but not fully hardened until then

cards.title: Day-to-day care
items: Wipe with a damp cloth and mild dish soap
Dry immediately — don't leave wet cloths against the surface
Use a microfiber cloth for fingerprints — no paper towels

cards.title: Annual check
items: Inspect hinge points and leading edges for any chips
Call for a touch-up visit at the first sign of edge chipping — early repair prevents spread
Check hardware tightness — loose hinges cause door-edge wear

cards.title: What to avoid
items: Ammonia-based cleaners (Windex, many multi-surface sprays)
Bleach or abrasive scrubbing pads
Steam directly from stovetop exhaust vented toward the cabinets
Adhesive hooks or contact paper stuck directly to the finish

warranty.heading: 2 years. Factory-grade. Backed by the owner.
covered: Finish adhesion failure — any delamination or peeling within 2 years, re-coated at no charge
Color inconsistency on delivered work — corrected before sign-off, not after
Hardware misalignment at final walkthrough — adjusted same day
notCovered: Chips or scratches from impact, dropped objects, or misuse after sign-off
Discoloration or damage from ammonia-based cleaners, bleach, or abrasive products
Normal surface wear over time — touch-up available at standard service rate

media.heroAlt: Cabinet doors receiving a 2K polyurethane spray finish
media.workbookPhotoAlt: Completed cabinet refinishing — Somerville condo kitchen
media.outcomeAfterAlt: Kitchen cabinets after 2K polyurethane finish — factory smooth

meta.title: Bathroom Refresh Planning Guide — Jerry & Co.
meta.description: What to check, select, and expect before a gut-free bathroom refresh. Tile, fixtures, vanity, and timeline — all in one place.

hero.headline: Everything you need to know before refreshing your bathroom without a gut
hero.subhead: New tile, fixtures, and paint without demolition or permit risk. This guide covers what to assess, what to select, and what to expect from start to finish.

selfAssessment.heading: 5 questions before you reach out
selfAssessment.intro: These determine whether a gut-free refresh is the right scope — or whether a full renovation is the better investment.

questions.q: Is the subfloor and substrate behind your existing tile in good condition — no soft spots, water damage, or visible mold?
questions.label: Verify first
questions.hint: If unknown, we assess this in the video walkthrough. Substrate issues can change the scope from refresh to full reno.

questions.q: Are you comfortable keeping plumbing supply and drain locations exactly where they are?
questions.label: Right for refresh
questions.hint: Moving supply or drain lines triggers permit requirements. A gut-free refresh works within the existing footprint — fast, compliant, no board approval needed in most buildings.

questions.q: Is your building ICC residential — most Boston, Cambridge, and Somerville condos built after 1980?
questions.label: Usually no permit needed
questions.hint: Like-for-like fixture and tile replacement typically doesn't require a permit in ICC residential classification. We confirm this in your building's specific context.

questions.q: Do you have a direction for the tile style and fixture finish — or are you starting from scratch?
questions.label: Good to think through
questions.hint: Not required before the estimate call. We walk through options together. Having a reference image or two helps us get to a quote faster.

questions.q: Can you be without this bathroom for 3–5 consecutive days?
questions.label: Plan ahead
questions.hint: If this is your only bathroom, we can stage the work to minimize disruption. We'll discuss logistics on the call.

selfAssessment.outcomeNote: Mostly green → great fit for a refresh. Yellow on substrate condition → we assess in the walkthrough. If we find issues that require a full gut, we'll scope that for you on the spot and let you decide.

preplanning.heading: What to document before the walkthrough
preplanning.intro: A few photos and measurements cut 20 minutes off the estimate call and get you a written quote faster.

categories.title: Your bathroom
items: Photograph all four walls, the floor, and the ceiling
Close-up of any grout discoloration, cracked tile, or damaged fixtures
Measure the floor footprint and note ceiling height
Note current tile size and format if visible

categories.title: Your building
items: Check whether your building requires a scope letter or COI before wet work
Note if neighboring units share a wet stack on this wall
Confirm contractor access hours and elevator reservation process
Ask if the building has a designated debris removal window

categories.title: Your selections
items: Save 2–3 tile references you like (Pinterest, Houzz, or manufacturer links)
Decide on a fixture finish direction — chrome, matte black, or brushed nickel
Think about vanity preference: refinish in 2K polyurethane, or replace
Note any items you want to keep (towel bars, mirror, toilet)

preplanning.callout: Clients who send bathroom photos in advance receive same-day written quotes 3x more often than those who arrive to the walkthrough without them.

decisions.heading: Decisions you'll make before work begins
decisions.intro: Refresh projects have more client-side selection than refinishing — this is where most of your input goes.

items.label: Floor tile
items.question: What goes on your bathroom floor?
options: 12×12 or larger format (fewest grout lines, modern look)
Hex mosaic (classic, pairs well with subway wall tile)
Large-format 24×24 (most contemporary, grout-joint minimal)
Porcelain wood-look plank (warm, durable)
items.jerrySays: Larger format tile makes small bathrooms feel bigger — it's counterintuitive but it works. For grip in a shower floor, go smaller and add grout joints. For a dry floor, large format reads cleaner.

items.label: Wall tile
items.question: What goes on your bathroom walls?
options: 3×6 subway (classic, works with any fixture finish)
4×12 or 4×16 subway (more current scale)
Large-format 12×24 or 18×36 (fewest grout lines, bold)
Full-height tile vs. tile to chair-rail height with paint above
items.jerrySays: Full-height subway is the safest choice for resale. If you're staying long-term, large-format with minimal grout lines is where design is heading and it photographs beautifully.

items.label: Fixtures
items.question: What fixture pieces are you replacing?
options: Toilet only
Vanity faucet and hardware only
Shower fixtures (head, valve trim, hand spray)
Full fixture package — toilet, faucet, shower, towel bars, mirror
items.jerrySays: Mixing fixture generations in the same bathroom ages poorly — if you're doing one, do them all in the same finish. Matte black and brushed nickel are most durable in humid environments; chrome shows water spots.

items.label: Vanity
items.question: What happens to the existing vanity?
options: Keep and refinish in 2K polyurethane to match or update the color
Keep and repaint (less durable, better for tight budgets)
Replace with a new vanity (adds most cost, full control of style)
items.jerrySays: 2K polyurethane on an existing vanity gets you a factory-grade finish for a fraction of the replacement cost. If the vanity box is solid and the size works, this is almost always the right call.

phases.label: Before we arrive
items: Tile, grout color, and fixture selections finalized and ordered
Vanity decision made — refinish or replace
Building scope letter or COI submitted if required
Elevator reservation and access window confirmed
You clear the bathroom of all personal items

phases.label: Days 1–5 in your unit
items: Day 1: Existing tile removal, debris staged and removed in one elevator window
Day 1–2: Substrate inspection, repair, and waterproofing application
Day 2–3: Floor tile set and grouted; wall tile set
Day 3–4: Wall tile grouted, fixtures installed, vanity finished
Day 5: Caulk lines, final touches, fixture function test, paint

phases.label: After sign-off
items: 24-hour grout cure before use — avoid moisture contact during this period
Final walkthrough: every grout line, fixture, and finish surface inspected together
Any touch-up items addressed same day before sign-off
Grout sealer applied at the 72-hour mark (we schedule a follow-up if needed)
Maintenance guide left with you — grout sealing schedule, fixture care

outcomes.heading: What 'done' actually looks like
gets: Fresh tile, grout, and fixtures installed to trade standard — tight lines, level plane, plumb fixtures
Vanity refinished or replaced to match the new palette
No permit triggered in most ICC residential buildings
Zero debris left in hallways or common areas
Grout sealed and fixture function tested before sign-off
know: Grout color shifts slightly as it cures over 28 days — the final color is lighter than it appears wet
Tile is set to existing substrate level — if substrate varies, there may be minor lippage on large-format tile
We cannot guarantee zero movement in tile over time in buildings with significant floor flex
Plumbing supply connection points are replaced but underlying supply line condition is outside scope

cards.title: Day-to-day care
items: Squeegee tile walls after every shower — it's the single best thing you can do for grout
Use a pH-neutral tile cleaner (avoid bleach-based sprays on colored grout)
Wipe vanity dry after use — standing water on the finish accelerates wear

cards.title: Annual check
items: Re-apply grout sealer every 12–18 months (we recommend a penetrating silicone sealer)
Inspect caulk lines at wall-floor junctions and around fixtures — re-caulk if cracking or separating
Check fixture connections under the vanity for any slow drip

cards.title: What to avoid
items: Bleach-based cleaners on unsealed or colored grout — causes discoloration
Abrasive scrubbing pads on any glazed tile surface
Leaving wet bath mats against the floor tile long-term — traps moisture under the mat

warranty.heading: Vanity finish, tile installation, fixtures — here's what holds and for how long.
covered: Tile installation defects — lippage beyond trade tolerance, cracked tile at delivery, grout voids
Fixture installation — leaks or improper function within 90 days of sign-off
Vanity finish adhesion failure within 2 years
notCovered: Grout cracking or discoloration from improper cleaning products or failure to seal on schedule
Tile cracking caused by building movement or subfloor deflection after sign-off
Fixture damage from improper use or third-party modification

media.heroAlt: Freshly tiled gut-free bathroom refresh with new fixtures
media.workbookPhotoAlt: Bathroom refresh in progress — tile and fixture installation
media.outcomeAfterAlt: Bathroom after gut-free tile and fixture refresh — Cambridge condo

meta.title: Interior Painting Planning Guide — Jerry & Co.
meta.description: What to prepare, decide, and expect before interior painting in your condo or home. Lead-safe, low-VOC, and done in 1–4 days.

hero.eyebrow: Planning guide · Lead-safe, low-VOC, condo-compliant painting
hero.headline: Interior painting for condos built before 1978 — lead-safe, low-VOC, and done in 1–4 days.
hero.subhead: Lead-safe, low-VOC, and contained within your unit. This guide covers everything from surface condition to sheen selection to what happens on production day.

selfAssessment.heading: 5 questions before you reach out
selfAssessment.intro: These flag anything that could change the scope or approach — and confirm whether painting is the right service for your space right now.

questions.q: Was your home built before 1978?
questions.label: Lead assessment required
questions.hint: Pre-1978 homes likely have lead paint. Jerry is EPA RRP certified — this is handled correctly and safely. Flag it now so we build it into the estimate.

questions.q: Are your walls in reasonable condition — no major holes, significant water staining, or actively peeling existing paint?
questions.label: Surface assessment
questions.hint: Minor holes and surface prep are included. Significant plaster damage or moisture-related peeling changes the scope — we assess this on the walkthrough.

questions.q: Do you know what you want to do with trim, doors, and ceiling — or are you focused on walls only?
questions.label: Scope decision
questions.hint: Walls only is the most common scope. Adding trim and doors adds 1–2 days. Knowing now helps us price accurately the first time.

questions.q: Is the unit occupied during the project?
questions.label: Plan for low-VOC
questions.hint: We use low-VOC formulas — safe for occupied units. You'll need to move furniture away from walls and have a plan for pets.

questions.q: Do you have a color direction in mind, or do you need help choosing?
questions.label: We can help
questions.hint: Color consultation is part of the estimate call. If you have references, bring them. If not, we'll narrow it down together.

selfAssessment.outcomeNote: Interior painting has the most flexible conditions of all our services. Most yellow items above are handled in the estimate call — they don't stop the project, they shape it.

preplanning.heading: What to document before the walkthrough
preplanning.intro: A quick walk-through of your space on video or in photos gives us what we need to build an accurate quote.

categories.title: Your space
items: Walk the rooms you want painted on video, or photograph each wall
Note ceiling heights — standard 8ft vs. vaulted affects pricing
Flag any water stains, cracks, or peeling areas with close-up photos
Note existing paint finish (flat, eggshell, gloss) if you know it

categories.title: Your building
items: Check condo bylaws for contractor access hours
Confirm elevator is available for equipment staging
Note whether you need to provide a COI to the building management
Pre-1978 building: ask your building records for any prior lead abatement documentation

categories.title: Your timeline
items: Painting is our most flexible service — 1–4 days depending on scope
Plan to have furniture moved away from walls the morning we start
If the unit is occupied, identify a space for displaced furniture temporarily
Color decisions must be final before we primer — no changes after prep begins

preplanning.callout: A short video walkthrough of your space — even filmed on your phone — gives us everything we need to quote remotely without an in-person visit.

decisions.heading: Decisions you'll make before work begins
decisions.intro: Fewer decisions than most services — but color and sheen are locked before the first coat goes on.

items.label: Color
items.question: What color or colors are going in each space?
options: Single color throughout (most cohesive, best value)
Accent wall in a contrasting color
Different colors per room
Ceiling in a lighter tint of the wall color
items.jerrySays: Test your color in the actual room before committing — the same swatch reads completely differently under north light vs. south light. We always recommend a sample pot first.

items.label: Sheen
items.question: What finish level goes where?
options: Flat — ceilings and low-traffic walls (hides imperfections best)
Eggshell — most interior walls (subtle sheen, wipeable)
Satin — kitchens, bathrooms, high-traffic areas
Semi-gloss — trim, doors, and casework (most durable, easiest to clean)
items.jerrySays: Eggshell for walls, flat for ceilings, semi-gloss for trim is the standard that works for most homes. Don't use flat on walls in a kitchen or bathroom — it won't hold up to wiping.

items.label: Scope
items.question: What surfaces are included in this project?
options: Walls only
Walls plus ceiling
Walls, ceiling, and trim (baseboards, door and window casings)
Full room — walls, ceiling, trim, and doors
items.jerrySays: If the trim is in good shape, walls-only gets you 80% of the visual impact at 60% of the cost. If the trim is scuffed and dingy, add it — fresh trim amplifies the wall color significantly.

items.label: Furniture
items.question: What needs to move before we start?
options: You'll move everything out of the rooms beforehand
Furniture stays — we work around and cover in place
Mix — some rooms cleared, some covered
items.jerrySays: Moving out gets you better results at corners and along baseboards. Covering in place is fine for larger pieces — we protect everything. Either way, walls behind furniture count toward the quote.

phases.label: Before we arrive
items: Color selections finalized — paint ordered or confirmed at the paint store
Scope locked: which rooms, which surfaces, which sheen levels
Lead assessment documented if pre-1978 building
Furniture plan confirmed — what moves, what gets covered
You'll receive a pre-arrival checklist 48 hours before start

phases.label: Days 1–4 in your unit
items: Day 1: Surface prep — patch holes, sand rough areas, caulk gaps at trim
Day 1: Primer coat on patched areas and any stained surfaces
Day 2: First finish coat on walls — edges cut in by hand before roller
Day 3: Second finish coat (most walls need two coats for full coverage)
Day 4 (if full scope): Trim and doors painted last to avoid lap marks on walls

phases.label: After sign-off
items: 1-hour dry-to-touch on most low-VOC formulas — light use same day
Full cure in 14–30 days — wait before washing walls or hanging items with adhesive
Final walkthrough: inspect edges, corners, and any patched areas together
Touch-up items addressed before sign-off — we leave a small touch-up quantity for future use
Furniture can return same day if paint is dry to the touch

outcomes.heading: What 'done' actually looks like
gets: Clean, consistent color across all specified surfaces — no holidays, lap marks, or uneven sheen
Patched areas blended into the surrounding surface — flush and invisible after paint
Lead-safe process documentation if applicable — we provide a certificate
Touch-up paint quantity left with you for future nicks and scuffs
know: Two coats are standard — very dark or very light colors over a contrasting base may need a third coat (discussed before, not after)
Fresh paint reads slightly different once fully cured at 30 days — it typically lightens and the sheen settles
Texture differences between patched and original surfaces can appear under raking light — we sand and feather to minimize, but perfect invisibility isn't guaranteed on older plaster
Wall color makes existing trim imperfections more visible — we note any trim issues in the walkthrough

maintenance.heading: Getting the most out of your paint job

cards.title: Day-to-day care
items: Use the touch-up paint we leave — a quick dab on a fresh scuff prevents the mark from setting
Wipe walls with a barely damp cloth — never soaking wet
Allow 30 days before washing walls with any cleaner

cards.title: Annual check
items: Inspect corner joints and trim caulk lines — re-caulk if cracking
Check around windows and exterior walls for any moisture-related bubbling
Touch up any areas with visible wear before they compound

cards.title: What to avoid
items: Magic Erasers on eggshell or flat finishes — they remove sheen and create dull patches
Hanging art with adhesive strips on walls for 30 days after painting
Steam from humidifiers or cooking directed at walls long-term

warranty.heading: Coverage / adhesion / color match — backed for 1 year.
covered: Coverage uniformity — any holidays, thin spots, or missed sections corrected before sign-off
Adhesion failure within 1 year — peeling or delamination on a properly prepared surface
Color match accuracy — if a mixed color is significantly off, we remix and re-coat at no charge
notCovered: Scuffs, marks, or damage from furniture, art, or normal use after sign-off
Peeling caused by underlying moisture that was present but not visible during prep
Color appearance under lighting conditions different from those during application

media.heroAlt: Freshly painted interior — clean walls and crisp trim in a Boston condo
media.workbookPhotoAlt: Interior painting in progress — fresh coat on walls
media.outcomeAfterAlt: Interior walls after professional painting — Arlington condo

meta.title: Full Bathroom Renovation Planning Guide — Jerry & Co.
meta.description: Complete planning guide for a full bathroom gut renovation. Self-assessment, tile and fixture decisions, permit navigation, day-by-day workbook, and warranty terms.

hero.eyebrow: Planning guide · Full gut renovation — permit navigation, board approval, and what it actually costs.
hero.headline: Everything to plan, decide, and expect before a full bathroom gut renovation
hero.subhead: This is our most complex scope — subfloor, waterproofing, tile, fixtures, and finish. This guide walks you through every decision and phase so nothing surprises you.

selfAssessment.heading: 5 questions before you reach out
selfAssessment.intro: A full renovation is a significant investment. These questions confirm it's the right scope — and flag anything that needs to be addressed upfront.

questions.q: Do you want a complete gut — removing all tile to the studs, replacing waterproofing membrane, and starting fresh?
questions.label: Right service
questions.hint: If yes, this is the right scope. If you're open to keeping the substrate and just refreshing surfaces, a gut-free refresh may be a better fit.

questions.q: Does your condo building require board approval or a permit for wet-area gut work?
questions.label: Plan ahead
questions.hint: Most Boston-area condo buildings require a scope letter and may require a permit for gut work affecting the wet stack. We help you navigate this — but it adds lead time before start.

questions.q: Have you identified a tile direction, fixture finish, and layout intent — or are you still in early exploration?
questions.label: Need to lock before quoting
questions.hint: A full renovation quote is highly selection-dependent. The estimate call helps you narrow decisions, but final tile and fixture selections must be confirmed before work begins.

questions.q: Is your plumbing stack in good condition — no known leaks, corrosion, or prior water damage in the walls?
questions.label: Assess in walkthrough
questions.hint: We assess this during the video walkthrough. Hidden supply line issues can change scope and budget — we surface them early, not mid-project.

questions.q: Are you prepared for a 5–12 day timeline with no access to this bathroom?
questions.label: Must plan ahead
questions.hint: If this is your only bathroom, we discuss logistics and staging options on the estimate call. Plan ahead — this is the one constraint that most affects scheduling.

selfAssessment.outcomeNote: Green on question 1 + 5 means you're ready to plan. Yellow items are project shaping inputs — we work through all of them in the walkthrough. If you're unsure about board approval or permits, bring your condo docs to the call and we'll review them together.

preplanning.heading: What to document before the walkthrough
preplanning.intro: A full renovation quote requires more detail than other services. The more you bring to the walkthrough, the more accurate and complete your quote will be.

categories.title: Your bathroom
items: Photograph all four walls, floor, ceiling, and inside the vanity cabinet
Video walk through slowly — we want to see the condition of every tile and grout line
Note any visible moisture staining, cracks, or soft spots in the wall near the shower
Measure the room dimensions — length, width, ceiling height, shower niche if applicable

categories.title: Your building
items: Pull your condo association bylaws — look for contractor access, wet work, and permit language
Confirm whether your unit has a private shut-off for the wet stack or requires a building engineer
Ask building management whether similar renovations in the building required permits
Identify the building's debris removal process — dumpster location or elevator window schedule

categories.title: Your design direction
items: Save 5–10 bathroom photos that represent the look you want — Pinterest, Houzz, Instagram
Identify your fixture finish preference: matte black, brushed nickel, polished chrome, or unlacquered brass
Decide on layout intent: keep plumbing locations as-is, or move something (move = permit threshold)
Think about lighting — are you replacing the fixture, and if so, do you need an electrician?

preplanning.callout: A full renovation estimate requires a complete video walkthrough — not just photos. Book a 20-minute FaceTime or video call and we'll scope the entire project in one session.

decisions.heading: Decisions you'll make before work begins
decisions.intro: Full renovations require the most client input of any scope we do. These decisions shape the budget, the permit requirement, and the production timeline.

items.label: Layout
items.question: Are you keeping the existing plumbing locations or moving anything?
options: Keep all plumbing exactly as-is — fastest, lowest cost, no permit in most cases
Move toilet or vanity supply only (minor repositioning)
Reconfigure shower or tub — moves drain or supply line
Full layout redesign — new footprint, new drain location
items.jerrySays: Keeping plumbing in place is the single biggest cost lever in a bathroom renovation. Even moving a toilet 12 inches can add $2,000–$4,000 and trigger a permit. Unless the current layout is genuinely unusable, keep it.

items.label: Tile package
items.question: What tile goes on the floor, walls, and shower enclosure?
options: Matching floor and wall tile (cohesive, fewer transitions)
Contrasting floor and wall tile (two distinct looks)
Full-height shower tile vs. partial height with painted wall above
Shower niche — location, size, and tile treatment
items.jerrySays: Order 10–15% more tile than the square footage requires — cuts and breakage are real. Discontinued tiles can't be back-ordered. Get the material before we demo the existing.

items.label: Fixtures
items.question: What fixture pieces are you replacing and in what finish?
options: Full package: toilet, vanity faucet, shower valve and trim, towel bars, mirror
Partial: toilet and shower only, keep vanity faucet
Premium: wall-hung toilet, thermostatic shower, freestanding vanity
Spec-to-budget: builder-grade fixtures for a flip or rental
items.jerrySays: Buy fixtures in the same finish family from one manufacturer — mixing brands creates subtle finish variation that's obvious in finished photos. Matte black is the easiest to maintain. Unlacquered brass is the hardest.

items.label: Waterproofing system
items.question: What waterproofing membrane goes behind the tile?
options: Schluter Kerdi system — bonded membrane over cement board
Redgard liquid-applied membrane over cement board
Wedi foam board system — integrated waterproofing panel
Durock + liquid waterproofing for budget builds
items.jerrySays: We specify Schluter or Redgard for all wet areas — both are warrantied systems with proven track records. We assess the existing substrate and recommend the right system for your specific walls and floor on the walkthrough.

items.label: Permit
items.question: Does your project require a permit?
options: Like-for-like replacement — no permit in most ICC residential buildings
Supply line relocation — may trigger permit
Drain relocation or structural change — permit required
Building board approval required regardless of permit status
items.jerrySays: We help you determine permit requirement based on your building classification and project scope. If a permit is needed, we handle the documentation and coordinate inspection. This adds 2–4 weeks to the pre-construction timeline.

phases.label: Before we arrive (2–4 weeks out)
items: Tile, fixture, and hardware selections finalized and ordered — allow 2–3 weeks for delivery
Permit applied for if required — we handle documentation, you provide condo association info
Board approval obtained if your building requires it
Building shut-off and access window scheduled with building management
You've cleared the bathroom and confirmed no-bathroom logistics for the project window

phases.label: Days 1–12 in your unit
items: Day 1: Full demolition — tile, fixtures, and any damaged substrate removed; debris staged and removed same day
Day 1–2: Subfloor inspection and repair; cement board or foam board installed
Day 2–3: Waterproofing membrane applied and tested
Day 3–5: Floor tile set, allowed to cure; wall tile set in sections
Day 5–7: Wall tile grouted; shower niche and transition trim installed
Day 7–9: Fixtures rough-in and final trim; vanity set and plumbed
Day 9–11: Paint, accessories, and final punch list
Day 12: Final inspection with permit inspector if applicable; final walkthrough

phases.label: After sign-off
items: 72-hour cure before grout sealer application — we schedule a follow-up or leave sealer with instructions
Final walkthrough: every tile, grout joint, fixture, and finish surface inspected together
Permit inspection completed if required — we coordinate and attend
All punch list items resolved before final sign-off — not after
Written warranty issued; copy of any permit or board approval documentation left with you

outcomes.heading: What 'done' actually looks like
gets: A fully waterproofed, trade-standard wet area from subfloor to ceiling
Tile set to level and plumb, grout consistent and sealed
All fixtures installed, plumbed, and function-tested before sign-off
Permit documentation and certificate of completion if applicable
A bathroom that photographs, sells, and holds up to daily use for 15+ years
know: Full renovations have inherent discovery risk — conditions behind the existing tile are not always visible until demo. We surface surprises early and present options before proceeding with any scope change
Permit and board approval timelines are outside our control — a 2-week permit process can become 4-6 weeks in certain municipalities
Tile and fixture lead times can affect project start — order early once selections are finalized
Grout color and sheen settle as they cure over 28 days — the day-1 appearance is not the final appearance

cards.title: Day-to-day care
items: Squeegee shower walls after every use — the single most effective thing you can do for grout longevity
Run the bathroom exhaust fan during every shower and for 20 minutes after
Use a pH-neutral cleaner on tile — never bleach-based products on colored grout

cards.title: Annual schedule
items: Re-seal grout every 12–18 months with a penetrating silicone sealer
Inspect all caulk lines — re-caulk at the first sign of cracking or mold
Check under-vanity connections for any slow drip at supply lines

cards.title: What to avoid
items: Harsh chemical drain cleaners — use enzymatic cleaners instead to protect the p-trap and grout joint at the drain
Leaving soap and shampoo bottles directly on the tile floor long-term — they stain grout
Ignoring a dripping faucet or running toilet — water intrusion is the enemy of a tile installation

warranty.heading: Waterproofing system / tile / fixtures / permit re-inspection — here's what we stand behind.
covered: Waterproofing system integrity — any failure within 2 years resulting in water intrusion through the tile assembly
Tile installation defects — grout voids, significant lippage, or cracked tile at delivery
Fixture installation — leaks or improper function within 90 days of sign-off
Permit inspection pass — we cover the cost of any required re-inspection if our work is the cause of a failed inspection
notCovered: Tile cracking caused by building settlement, seismic activity, or subfloor deflection after sign-off
Grout discoloration caused by failure to seal on schedule or use of incompatible cleaning products
Plumbing supply line failure beyond the shutoff — this is a building or pre-existing condition
Manufacturer defects in fixtures or tile — covered by their respective manufacturer warranties
